A frigid Arctic airmass settled over the area bringing very low wind chill values over northwest and central North Dakota, which was followed by an even colder Arctic airmass, resulting in extremely cold wind chill values throughout all of western and central North Dakota. The lowest wind chill values were experienced the night of January 19 into the morning of January 20, when almost all of western and central North Dakota experienced minimum wind chill values of 45 below to around 60 below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dropped as low as 60 below.

The lowest wind chill values were experienced the night of January 19 into the morning of January 20, when almost all of western and central North Dakota experienced minimum wind chill values of 45 below to around 60 below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dropped as low as 60 below.

On the morning of the 17th an arctic front pushed across the area creating white out conditions with blowing snow as winds gusted over 60 MPH in the northern valley and northeastern ND. This storm brought Blizzard conditions to Cavalier, Towner and Pembina counties where there was enough snowpack to blow around with winds measured as high as 62mph in Langdon. To the south less snow kept conditions from causing a widespread blizzard but winds still gusted over 58 mph in potions of Grand Forks, Benson, Eddy, Walsh and even as far south as Cass County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions observed upstream continuously in Rolla since 7am with supporting webcam verification in NDAWN mesonet in Perth.

Only a couple days after the Arctic airmass departed, well above average temperatures spread across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chill values dropped to between 45 below to around 60 below over most locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving through southeast Manitoba into southwest Ontario resulted in gusty west to northwest winds over much of North Dakota. Over north central North Dakota, gusts to 40 mph combined with occasional falling snow and snow on the ground to produce widespread blowing snow. Visibility reduced to under one-quarter mile producing blizzard conditions during the morning hours. The blizzard continued until mid-afternoon when visibility improved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling snow and snow on the ground to produce a blizzard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted surface trough combined with a frontal boundary and an upper level short wave trough to produce snow over much of western and central North Dakota, with the heaviest snow remaining south of Interstate 94. The highest snow reported was 11 inches near Selfridge in Sioux County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mott received six inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frigid Arctic airmass settled over the region bringing very low wind chills to western and central North Dakota, with most locations experiencing minimum wind chill values of 45 below to around 60 below. Only a couple days after the Arctic airmass departed, well above average temperatures spread across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chill values dropped to between 45 below to around 60 below over most locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A variety of weak short-wave troughs moving through southwest flow aloft combined with a slow moving frontal boundary to produced thunderstorms over parts of western and central North Dakota.  The thunderstorms were very short-lived, but were able to produce brief severe wind gusts over southwest North Dakota due to a very dry lower atmosphere. The strongest reported wind gust of 77 mph occurred at the Hettinger Airport in Adams County.

Severe to extreme drought conditions, D2-D3, continued into the month of May over much of western into parts of central North Dakota. Very limited changes occurred through mid-May, but widespread rains over central into parts of western North Dakota led to some improvement. The most notable improvement was to the eastern portions of the drought area where heavier rains occurred, with Adams, Hettinger, and Mercer Counties improving from D2 (Severe Drought) to D1 (Moderate Drought).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Friday, June 20th into the early morning hours of June 21st, much of the region was affected by severe weather. Supercell thunderstorms developed across southeast North Dakota ahead of an approaching convective complex, producing multiple strong, destructive, and fatal tornadoes. This was followed by a powerful line of severe thunderstorms categorized as a derecho, which tracked across much of the region resulting in widespread straight line wind damage. After the derecho passed through the region, there was a period of prolonged destructive winds that affected portions of central and east central North Dakota, as well as adjacent portions of Minnesota. This was a unique event due to the combination of the violent tornadoes ahead of the derecho, and the long duration of significant winds that followed the derecho. Based on estimates provided by North Dakota Agriculture Commissioner Doug Goehring, the state lost anywhere from 50 to 80 million bushels of grain storage capacity from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of a 92 mph wind gust 4ENE of Thompson. Measured on a Davis weather station.

In late April Williams, Divide, Burke, and Renville Counties improved from D2 to D1.

Two rounds of snow fell over western and central North Dakota. The first round of snow was due to an initial short-wave trough passing over the area, with the second round of snow due to the main long-wave trough moving over. The heaviest snow from these systems occurred over far south central North Dakota into the James River Valley. The greatest snow amount reported was 12 inches in the city of LaMoure in LaMoure County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: LaMoure received 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stacked low pressure system over central North Dakota resulted in thunderstorms moving through the area, a few of which produced small hail. One storm produced a funnel cloud near Heaton in Wells County.
A supercell developed in far southern Richland County near the South Dakota border, producing an EF-1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This slow moving tornado started in a marshy low lying field and tracked east then northeast encountering a grove of trees. Here the tornado snapped off many tree trunks and continued to the northeast with scouring occurring in the corn field. It encountered another grove of trees snapping more large branches and tree trunks before lifting near 101st Street SE. Drone footage shared by emergency management was used for the survey. Max wind speed is estimated at 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed as a cold front moved through central and eastern North Dakota. One thunderstorm produced nickel size hail over Stutsman County.

An environment with elevated instability and very strong deep layer shear set the stage for severe weather development over western and south central North Dakota. A long lived supercell thunderstorm developed over far southwest North Dakota near a modest frontal boundary, and quickly became severe over Billings County. The storm then continued to track to the east-southeast producing very large damaging hail as it moved through Stark, Hettinger, and Grant Counties. The largest hail reported was three inches in diameter which fell south of Dickinson in Stark County. In addition, a tornado developed approximately 9 miles southwest of Dickinson in Stark County, and traveled to the south approximately 1.4 miles. Two houses along the path of the tornado had roofs partially blown off and some siding ripped off. Tree damage also occurred, with one tree having an eight inch diameter branch snap off. Fences were broken down and two power poles were broken. Based on the damage done this tornado was rated EF-1, and from that wind speeds were estimated to be around 100 mph. Additional thunderstorms developed and moved through central and western North Dakota through the evening. One of these storms became severe over south central North Dakota producing one-inch diameter hail in Logan County.

Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours over central North Dakota in a very unstable environment, with several becoming supercells. Initially, very large hail was the main threat, but as the episode continued, tornadic development became the primary concern from any storms, with a total of nine tornadoes occurring. The first tornado touched down southwest of Hannover in Oliver County, and less than an hour later another tornado developed north of Crown Butte Lake in northeast Morton County. Then, after approximately an hour and a half, seven tornadoes were reported in the span of one hour, from around 10:12 to 11:12 PM CDT, in Burleigh, Kidder, and Emmons Counties. The most damaging tornado, which was rated an EF-2, impacted a farmstead west of Temvik in Emmons County causing significant damage. Two notable tornadoes touched down just to the north of the city of Bismarck which were highly visible, but one only caused limited damage. In addition to the tornadoes, these storms produced very large hail. The largest hail reported was 3.5 inches in diameter in Oliver County near Hannover, which fell from the same storm that produced the first tornado in Oliver County. North of Bismarck, extensive damage occurred due to hail up to the size of baseballs falling in multiple rural subdivisions earlier in the evening before the tornadic storm arrived. Many hail reports were received from the ICECHIP (In-situ Collaborative Experiment for the Collection of Hail In the Plains) project in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outbreak of severe thunderstorms brought extreme winds, very large hail, and multiple tornadoes across much of southern North Dakota from the late afternoon into the nighttime hours. A low-pressure system in southeastern Montana and a warm front stretching eastward across southern North Dakota provided a focus for the severe thunderstorms. Several environmental parameters were in place for a significant outbreak, most notable was the amount of deep-layer wind shear. The lowest layer of the atmosphere (up to 1 mile above the surface) also contained strong wind shear, which was one of the key ingredients that resulted in the higher risk for strong tornadoes. This event has been classified as a derecho, a term that is reserved for the most intense, widespread, and long-lasting lines of severe thunderstorms. By definition, a derecho is a widespread severe windstorm characterized by a family of destructive downbursts containing multiple 75+ mph gusts associated with the system.|Thunderstorms initially developed in eastern Montana in the late afternoon and moved into southwest North Dakota. Severe wind gusts around 60 mph were reported in far southwest North Dakota from Dickinson to Bowman during the early evening. Thereafter, storms grew into a pronounced line as they moved east and began accelerating. Shortly thereafter, extremely strong wind gusts started occurring, including a measured 94 mph gust to the northwest of Elgin in Grant County, and estimated 95 mph wind gusts in Hettinger County where a farmstead was severely damaged. Meanwhile, a supercell thunderstorm developed just south of Jamestown, and produced three-inch diameter hail as it moved northward into the city. This storm then slowly moved to the east-northeast and produced a brief tornado between Jamestown and Spiritwood. Shortly thereafter, a larger tornado formed near Spiritwood and moved to the east-northeast into Barnes County where EF-3 damage occurred, before tracking back into Stutsman County while it was dissipating. Specific details on the damage with this event can be found in the National Weather Service-Grand Forks Storm Data entry. Another supercell thunderstorm then formed south of the initial storm, moving southeast through southern Stutsman and northern LaMoure Counties. This storm produced a tornado near Adrian and another north of Verona. During this time, the line of storms to the west continued to strengthen, producing a wide swath of 80 to 100 mph wind gusts as it moved through south central North Dakota and across the James River Valley during the mid-evening into the nighttime hours. The derecho caused extensive tree and structure damage along its path, with significant damage to grain storage equipment and power transmission poles. The highest measured wind gust associated with the derecho was 101 mph at a North Dakota Agricultural Weather Network (NDAWN) station 5 miles northwest of Linton. There were also several tornadoes produced by the intense line of storms, including two tornadoes rated as EF-1 (north of Interstate 94 between Crystal Springs and Medina near the Kidder/Stutsman County Line, and to the southwest of Grace City in Foster County), and one tornado rated as EF-2 (north of Sweet Briar Lake in Morton County).  The derecho resulted in one injury which occurred in a housing development to the south of Lincoln in Burleigh County as a male was outdoors and was struck by flying debris. Also, one indirect fatality occurred near Courtenay in Stutsman County where a 76-year-old male died in his sleep from his breathing device not operating properly due to the power outage caused by the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado briefly touched down southwest of Spiritwood from the storm that eventually produced the damaging tornado that developed east of Spiritwood and tracked into Barnes County. This occurred in an open field and impacted no structures. There was no damage to evaluate. By National Weather Service policy the tornado is rated EF-U (Unknown).

In addition, a tornado developed approximately 9 miles southwest of Dickinson in Stark County, and traveled to the south approximately 1.4 miles. Two houses along the path of the tornado had roofs partially blown off and some siding ripped off. Tree damage also occurred, with one tree having an eight inch diameter branch snap off. Fences were broken down and two power poles were broken. Based on the damage done this tornado was rated EF-1, and from that wind speeds were estimated to be around 100 mph. Additional thunderstorms developed and moved through central and western North Dakota through the evening. One of these storms became severe over south central North Dakota producing one-inch diameter hail in Logan County.

Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours over central North Dakota in a very unstable environment, with several becoming supercells. Initially, very large hail was the main threat, but as the episode continued, tornadic development became the primary concern from any storms, with a total of nine tornadoes occurring. The first tornado touched down southwest of Hannover in Oliver County, and less than an hour later another tornado developed north of Crown Butte Lake in northeast Morton County. Then, after approximately an hour and a half, seven tornadoes were reported in the span of one hour, from around 10:12 to 11:12 PM CDT, in Burleigh, Kidder, and Emmons Counties. The most damaging tornado, which was rated an EF-2, impacted a farmstead west of Temvik in Emmons County causing significant damage. Two notable tornadoes touched down just to the north of the city of Bismarck which were highly visible, but one only caused limited damage. In addition to the tornadoes, these storms produced very large hail. The largest hail reported was 3.5 inches in diameter in Oliver County near Hannover, which fell from the same storm that produced the first tornado in Oliver County. North of Bismarck, extensive damage occurred due to hail up to the size of baseballs falling in multiple rural subdivisions earlier in the evening before the tornadic storm arrived. Many hail reports were received from the ICECHIP (In-situ Collaborative Experiment for the Collection of Hail In the Plains) project in the area.

An outbreak of severe thunderstorms brought extreme winds, very large hail, and multiple tornadoes across much of southern North Dakota from the late afternoon into the nighttime hours. A low-pressure system in southeastern Montana and a warm front stretching eastward across southern North Dakota provided a focus for the severe thunderstorms. Several environmental parameters were in place for a significant outbreak, most notable was the amount of deep-layer wind shear. The lowest layer of the atmosphere (up to 1 mile above the surface) also contained strong wind shear, which was one of the key ingredients that resulted in the higher risk for strong tornadoes. This event has been classified as a derecho, a term that is reserved for the most intense, widespread, and long-lasting lines of severe thunderstorms. By definition, a derecho is a widespread severe windstorm characterized by a family of destructive downbursts containing multiple 75+ mph gusts associated with the system.|Thunderstorms initially developed in eastern Montana in the late afternoon and moved into southwest North Dakota. Severe wind gusts around 60 mph were reported in far southwest North Dakota from Dickinson to Bowman during the early evening. Thereafter, storms grew into a pronounced line as they moved east and began accelerating. Shortly thereafter, extremely strong wind gusts started occurring, including a measured 94 mph gust to the northwest of Elgin in Grant County, and estimated 95 mph wind gusts in Hettinger County where a farmstead was severely damaged. Meanwhile, a supercell thunderstorm developed just south of Jamestown, and produced three-inch diameter hail as it moved northward into the city. This storm then slowly moved to the east-northeast and produced a brief tornado between Jamestown and Spiritwood. Shortly thereafter, a larger tornado formed near Spiritwood and moved to the east-northeast into Barnes County where EF-3 damage occurred, before tracking back into Stutsman County while it was dissipating. Specific details on the damage with this event can be found in the National Weather Service-Grand Forks Storm Data entry. Another supercell thunderstorm then formed south of the initial storm, moving southeast through southern Stutsman and northern LaMoure Counties. This storm produced a tornado near Adrian and another north of Verona. During this time, the line of storms to the west continued to strengthen, producing a wide swath of 80 to 100 mph wind gusts as it moved through south central North Dakota and across the James River Valley during the mid-evening into the nighttime hours. The derecho caused extensive tree and structure damage along its path, with significant damage to grain storage equipment and power transmission poles. The highest measured wind gust associated with the derecho was 101 mph at a North Dakota Agricultural Weather Network (NDAWN) station 5 miles northwest of Linton. There were also several tornadoes produced by the intense line of storms, including two tornadoes rated as EF-1 (north of Interstate 94 between Crystal Springs and Medina near the Kidder/Stutsman County Line, and to the southwest of Grace City in Foster County), and one tornado rated as EF-2 (north of Sweet Briar Lake in Morton County).  The derecho resulted in one injury which occurred in a housing development to the south of Lincoln in Burleigh County as a male was outdoors and was struck by flying debris. Also, one indirect fatality occurred near Courtenay in Stutsman County where a 76-year-old male died in his sleep from his breathing device not operating properly due to the power outage caused by the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated by a trained spotter.

An environment with elevated instability and very strong deep layer shear set the stage for severe weather development over western and south central North Dakota. A long lived supercell thunderstorm developed over far southwest North Dakota near a modest frontal boundary, and quickly became severe over Billings County. The storm then continued to track to the east-southeast producing very large damaging hail as it moved through Stark, Hettinger, and Grant Counties. The largest hail reported was three inches in diameter which fell south of Dickinson in Stark County. In addition, a tornado developed approximately 9 miles southwest of Dickinson in Stark County, and traveled to the south approximately 1.4 miles. Two houses along the path of the tornado had roofs partially blown off and some siding ripped off. Tree damage also occurred, with one tree having an eight inch diameter branch snap off. Fences were broken down and two power poles were broken. Based on the damage done this tornado was rated EF-1, and from that wind speeds were estimated to be around 100 mph. Additional thunderstorms developed and moved through central and western North Dakota through the evening. One of these storms became severe over south central North Dakota producing one-inch diameter hail in Logan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down approximately 9 miles southwest of Dickinson and traveled to the south approximately 1.4 miles. Two houses along the path of the tornado had roofs partially blown off and some siding ripped off.  Tree damage also occurred, with one tree having an eight inch diameter branch snapped off. Fences were broken down and two power poles were broken. Based on the damage done this tornado was rated EF-1, and from that wind speeds were estimated to be around 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours over central North Dakota in a very unstable environment, with several becoming supercells. Initially, very large hail was the main threat, but as the episode continued, tornadic development became the primary concern from any storms, with a total of nine tornadoes occurring. The first tornado touched down southwest of Hannover in Oliver County, and less than an hour later another tornado developed north of Crown Butte Lake in northeast Morton County. Then, after approximately an hour and a half, seven tornadoes were reported in the span of one hour, from around 10:12 to 11:12 PM CDT, in Burleigh, Kidder, and Emmons Counties. The most damaging tornado, which was rated an EF-2, impacted a farmstead west of Temvik in Emmons County causing significant damage. Two notable tornadoes touched down just to the north of the city of Bismarck which were highly visible, but one only caused limited damage. In addition to the tornadoes, these storms produced very large hail. The largest hail reported was 3.5 inches in diameter in Oliver County near Hannover, which fell from the same storm that produced the first tornado in Oliver County. North of Bismarck, extensive damage occurred due to hail up to the size of baseballs falling in multiple rural subdivisions earlier in the evening before the tornadic storm arrived. Many hail reports were received from the ICECHIP (In-situ Collaborative Experiment for the Collection of Hail In the Plains) project in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours over central North Dakota in a very unstable environment, with several becoming supercells. Initially, very large hail was the main threat, but as the episode continued, tornadic development became the primary concern from any storms, with a total of nine tornadoes occurring. The first tornado touched down southwest of Hannover in Oliver County, and less than an hour later another tornado developed north of Crown Butte Lake in northeast Morton County. Then, after approximately an hour and a half, seven tornadoes were reported in the span of one hour, from around 10:12 to 11:12 PM CDT, in Burleigh, Kidder, and Emmons Counties. The most damaging tornado, which was rated an EF-2, impacted a farmstead west of Temvik in Emmons County causing significant damage. Two notable tornadoes touched down just to the north of the city of Bismarck which were highly visible, but one only caused limited damage. In addition to the tornadoes, these storms produced very large hail. The largest hail reported was 3.5 inches in diameter in Oliver County near Hannover, which fell from the same storm that produced the first tornado in Oliver County. North of Bismarck, extensive damage occurred due to hail up to the size of baseballs falling in multiple rural subdivisions earlier in the evening before the tornadic storm arrived. Many hail reports were received from the ICECHIP (In-situ Collaborative Experiment for the Collection of Hail In the Plains) project in the area.

Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours over central North Dakota in a very unstable environment, with several becoming supercells. Initially, very large hail was the main threat, but as the episode continued, tornadic development became the primary concern from any storms, with a total of nine tornadoes occurring. The first tornado touched down southwest of Hannover in Oliver County, and less than an hour later another tornado developed north of Crown Butte Lake in northeast Morton County. Then, after approximately an hour and a half, seven tornadoes were reported in the span of one hour, from around 10:12 to 11:12 PM CDT, in Burleigh, Kidder, and Emmons Counties. The most damaging tornado, which was rated an EF-2, impacted a farmstead west of Temvik in Emmons County causing significant damage. Two notable tornadoes touched down just to the north of the city of Bismarck which were highly visible, but one only caused limited damage. In addition to the tornadoes, these storms produced very large hail. The largest hail reported was 3.5 inches in diameter in Oliver County near Hannover, which fell from the same storm that produced the first tornado in Oliver County. North of Bismarck, extensive damage occurred due to hail up to the size of baseballs falling in multiple rural subdivisions earlier in the evening before the tornadic storm arrived. Many hail reports were received from the ICECHIP (In-situ Collaborative Experiment for the Collection of Hail In the Plains) project in the area.

An outbreak of severe thunderstorms brought extreme winds, very large hail, and multiple tornadoes across much of southern North Dakota from the late afternoon into the nighttime hours. Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours over central North Dakota in a very unstable environment, with several becoming supercells. Initially, very large hail was the main threat, but as the episode continued, tornadic development became the primary concern from any storms, with a total of nine tornadoes occurring. The first tornado touched down southwest of Hannover in Oliver County, and less than an hour later another tornado developed north of Crown Butte Lake in northeast Morton County. Then, after approximately an hour and a half, seven tornadoes were reported in the span of one hour, from around 10:12 to 11:12 PM CDT, in Burleigh, Kidder, and Emmons Counties. The most damaging tornado, which was rated an EF-2, impacted a farmstead west of Temvik in Emmons County causing significant damage. Two notable tornadoes touched down just to the north of the city of Bismarck which were highly visible, but one only caused limited damage. In addition to the tornadoes, these storms produced very large hail. The largest hail reported was 3.5 inches in diameter in Oliver County near Hannover, which fell from the same storm that produced the first tornado in Oliver County. North of Bismarck, extensive damage occurred due to hail up to the size of baseballs falling in multiple rural subdivisions earlier in the evening before the tornadic storm arrived. Many hail reports were received from the ICECHIP (In-situ Collaborative Experiment for the Collection of Hail In the Plains) project in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A window was broken, roofing was damaged, and the shop and barn were severely dented.

Shortly thereafter, extremely strong wind gusts started occurring, including a measured 94 mph gust to the northwest of Elgin in Grant County, and estimated 95 mph wind gusts in Hettinger County where a farmstead was severely damaged. Meanwhile, a supercell thunderstorm developed just south of Jamestown, and produced three-inch diameter hail as it moved northward into the city. This storm then slowly moved to the east-northeast and produced a brief tornado between Jamestown and Spiritwood. Shortly thereafter, a larger tornado formed near Spiritwood and moved to the east-northeast into Barnes County where EF-3 damage occurred, before tracking back into Stutsman County while it was dissipating. Specific details on the damage with this event can be found in the National Weather Service-Grand Forks Storm Data entry. Another supercell thunderstorm then formed south of the initial storm, moving southeast through southern Stutsman and northern LaMoure Counties. This storm produced a tornado near Adrian and another north of Verona. During this time, the line of storms to the west continued to strengthen, producing a wide swath of 80 to 100 mph wind gusts as it moved through south central North Dakota and across the James River Valley during the mid-evening into the nighttime hours. The derecho caused extensive tree and structure damage along its path, with significant damage to grain storage equipment and power transmission poles. The highest measured wind gust associated with the derecho was 101 mph at a North Dakota Agricultural Weather Network (NDAWN) station 5 miles northwest of Linton. There were also several tornadoes produced by the intense line of storms, including two tornadoes rated as EF-1 (north of Interstate 94 between Crystal Springs and Medina near the Kidder/Stutsman County Line, and to the southwest of Grace City in Foster County), and one tornado rated as EF-2 (north of Sweet Briar Lake in Morton County).  The derecho resulted in one injury which occurred in a housing development to the south of Lincoln in Burleigh County as a male was outdoors and was struck by flying debris. Also, one indirect fatality occurred near Courtenay in Stutsman County where a 76-year-old male died in his sleep from his breathing device not operating properly due to the power outage caused by the storm.

Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours over central North Dakota in a very unstable environment, with several becoming supercells. Initially, very large hail was the main threat, but as the episode continued, tornadic development became the primary concern from any storms, with a total of nine tornadoes occurring. The first tornado touched down southwest of Hannover in Oliver County, and less than an hour later another tornado developed north of Crown Butte Lake in northeast Morton County. Then, after approximately an hour and a half, seven tornadoes were reported in the span of one hour, from around 10:12 to 11:12 PM CDT, in Burleigh, Kidder, and Emmons Counties. The most damaging tornado, which was rated an EF-2, impacted a farmstead west of Temvik in Emmons County causing significant damage. Two notable tornadoes touched down just to the north of the city of Bismarck which were highly visible, but one only caused limited damage. In addition to the tornadoes, these storms produced very large hail. The largest hail reported was 3.5 inches in diameter in Oliver County near Hannover, which fell from the same storm that produced the first tornado in Oliver County. North of Bismarck, extensive damage occurred due to hail up to the size of baseballs falling in multiple rural subdivisions earlier in the evening before the tornadic storm arrived. Many hail reports were received from the ICECHIP (In-situ Collaborative Experiment for the Collection of Hail In the Plains) project in the area.

North of Bismarck, extensive damage occurred due to hail up to the size of baseballs falling in multiple rural subdivisions earlier in the evening before the tornadic storm arrived. Many hail reports were received from the ICECHIP (In-situ Collaborative Experiment for the Collection of Hail In the Plains) project in the area.

Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours over central North Dakota in a very unstable environment, with several becoming supercells. Initially, very large hail was the main threat, but as the episode continued, tornadic development became the primary concern from any storms, with a total of nine tornadoes occurring. The first tornado touched down southwest of Hannover in Oliver County, and less than an hour later another tornado developed north of Crown Butte Lake in northeast Morton County. Then, after approximately an hour and a half, seven tornadoes were reported in the span of one hour, from around 10:12 to 11:12 PM CDT, in Burleigh, Kidder, and Emmons Counties. The most damaging tornado, which was rated an EF-2, impacted a farmstead west of Temvik in Emmons County causing significant damage. Two notable tornadoes touched down just to the north of the city of Bismarck which were highly visible, but one only caused limited damage. In addition to the tornadoes, these storms produced very large hail. The largest hail reported was 3.5 inches in diameter in Oliver County near Hannover, which fell from the same storm that produced the first tornado in Oliver County. North of Bismarck, extensive damage occurred due to hail up to the size of baseballs falling in multiple rural subdivisions earlier in the evening before the tornadic storm arrived. Many hail reports were received from the ICECHIP (In-situ Collaborative Experiment for the Collection of Hail In the Plains) project in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees were uprooted and were laying across a road.

A QLCS type mesoscale convective system brought widespread severe thunderstorm winds to eastern ND, along with, multiple brief tornadoes during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This rain wrapped QLCS tornado developed about 4 miles east of the community of Kempton or 5.5 miles northeast of Northwood where it initially caused crop damage, seen via high resolution satellite. As it tracked east, it tipped over an irrigation pivot system, and caused EF-1 damage to trees along shelterbelts. It also caused  EF1 tree damage and minor damage to a property as it turned northeast. It then finally produced more crop damage based on high resolution satellite before lifting. Peak wind speed is estimated at 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A QLCS type mesoscale convective system brought widespread severe thunderstorm winds to eastern ND, along with, multiple brief tornadoes during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This QLCS tornado was very brief with damage confined to one field producing EF-0 damage. The sunflowers were flattened in a very short concentric and convergent path.  TDS observed on radar. Time estimated from KMVX radar. Max wind speed estimated at 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A QLCS type mesoscale convective system brought widespread severe thunderstorm winds to eastern ND, along with, multiple brief tornadoes during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This QLCS tornado was very brief with EF-0 damage confined to one field and a shelterbelt. The sunflowers were flattened in a very short concentric and convergent path. Time estimated from KMVX radar. TDS observed on radar. Max wind speed estimated at 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A QLCS type mesoscale convective system brought widespread severe thunderstorm winds to eastern ND, along with, multiple brief tornadoes during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The track of this tornado is seen on high resolution satellite imagery, with mainly crop damage as it tracked northeast and eventually caused large tree branches to be snapped in a shelterbelt before lifting. TDS observed on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A QLCS type mesoscale convective system brought widespread severe thunderstorm winds to eastern ND, along with, multiple brief tornadoes during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This brief QLCS tornado caused EF-1 damage to large cottonwood trees which were uprooted and snapped halfway up the truck. As it continued tracking northeast it caused crop damage and additional EF-1 damage to trees before lifting. Time estimated from KVX radar. TDS observed on radar. Sentinel-2 high resolution satellite imagery reveals crop damage. Max wind speed is estimated at 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A QLCS type mesoscale convective system brought widespread severe thunderstorm winds to eastern ND, along with, multiple brief tornadoes during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed about 2.8 miles east of Hatton and tracked northeast. It damaged a farmstead where the garage door of large shed was torn off, a tree was uprooted, and  numerous large branches were broken. Numerous large tree branches snapped and uprooted along path as it tracked northeast crossed multiple shelterbelts. Time estimated from KVX radar. TDS observed on radar. Sentinel-2 high resolution satellite imagery reveals crop damage along the path. Maximum wind speed is estimated at 104 mph based on the tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A QLCS type mesoscale convective system brought widespread severe thunderstorm winds to eastern ND, along with, multiple brief tornadoes during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This brief tornado developed about 1.7 miles west of Red Willow Lake and caused damage to crops. High resolution satellite imagery reveals ground scouring and crop damage along the short narrow path. Time estimated from KMVX radar. Maximum wind is estimated at 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A QLCS type mesoscale convective system brought widespread severe thunderstorm winds to eastern ND, along with, multiple brief tornadoes during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This QLCS tornado was likely rain wrapped with the starting location approximated based on high resolution satellite imagery with scouring occurring in a wheat field. The tornado moved to the northeast and destroyed a 120 ft by 60 ft pole barn. Max estimated wind is 110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outbreak of severe thunderstorms brought extreme winds, very large hail, and multiple tornadoes across much of southern North Dakota from the late afternoon into the nighttime hours. A low-pressure system in southeastern Montana and a warm front stretching eastward across southern North Dakota provided a focus for the severe thunderstorms. Several environmental parameters were in place for a significant outbreak, most notable was the amount of deep-layer wind shear. The lowest layer of the atmosphere (up to 1 mile above the surface) also contained strong wind shear, which was one of the key ingredients that resulted in the higher risk for strong tornadoes. This event has been classified as a derecho, a term that is reserved for the most intense, widespread, and long-lasting lines of severe thunderstorms. By definition, a derecho is a widespread severe windstorm characterized by a family of destructive downbursts containing multiple 75+ mph gusts associated with the system.|Thunderstorms initially developed in eastern Montana in the late afternoon and moved into southwest North Dakota. Severe wind gusts around 60 mph were reported in far southwest North Dakota from Dickinson to Bowman during the early evening. Thereafter, storms grew into a pronounced line as they moved east and began accelerating. Shortly thereafter, extremely strong wind gusts started occurring, including a measured 94 mph gust to the northwest of Elgin in Grant County, and estimated 95 mph wind gusts in Hettinger County where a farmstead was severely damaged. Meanwhile, a supercell thunderstorm developed just south of Jamestown, and produced three-inch diameter hail as it moved northward into the city. This storm then slowly moved to the east-northeast and produced a brief tornado between Jamestown and Spiritwood. Shortly thereafter, a larger tornado formed near Spiritwood and moved to the east-northeast into Barnes County where EF-3 damage occurred, before tracking back into Stutsman County while it was dissipating. Specific details on the damage with this event can be found in the National Weather Service-Grand Forks Storm Data entry. Another supercell thunderstorm then formed south of the initial storm, moving southeast through southern Stutsman and northern LaMoure Counties. This storm produced a tornado near Adrian and another north of Verona. During this time, the line of storms to the west continued to strengthen, producing a wide swath of 80 to 100 mph wind gusts as it moved through south central North Dakota and across the James River Valley during the mid-evening into the nighttime hours. The derecho caused extensive tree and structure damage along its path, with significant damage to grain storage equipment and power transmission poles. The highest measured wind gust associated with the derecho was 101 mph at a North Dakota Agricultural Weather Network (NDAWN) station 5 miles northwest of Linton. There were also several tornadoes produced by the intense line of storms, including two tornadoes rated as EF-1 (north of Interstate 94 between Crystal Springs and Medina near the Kidder/Stutsman County Line, and to the southwest of Grace City in Foster County), and one tornado rated as EF-2 (north of Sweet Briar Lake in Morton County).  The derecho resulted in one injury which occurred in a housing development to the south of Lincoln in Burleigh County as a male was outdoors and was struck by flying debris. Also, one indirect fatality occurred near Courtenay in Stutsman County where a 76-year-old male died in his sleep from his breathing device not operating properly due to the power outage caused by the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees near the house and in a nearby shelter belt uprooted or snapped. Small shed demolished. Roof partially torn off of a small horse shed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the first event entry for one tornado that passed through two counties, Kidder and Stutsman. This Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) tornado started northwest of Crystal Springs in Kidder County and moved to the east-southeast. The tornado then shifted towards the east-northeast near the Stutsman County line, and continued into Stutsman County before it lifted to the north-northwest of Medina. Near the point of touchdown, a machine shed was completely destroyed, with the roof laying in a pasture about 350 feet away. Approximately five miles to the south-southeast, two additional farmsteads in close proximity were struck. The first one had an older barn along with a pole barn completely destroyed. The second farmstead had an older barn collapse, and an empty 18 foot diameter grain bin lifted and moved approximately 400 feet. Along the grain bin's path it hit a building, two vehicles, and a camper before it came to rest in front of the collapsed barn. Shortly thereafter, the tornado turned north-northeast and then struck another farmstead approximately 3 miles away. At this farm, a quonset building was moved off its foundation, and boards from the inside were sucked out and laying a few feet away. Additional buildings were damaged, with debris from a children's play house blown a half mile away and a picnic table blown a quarter mile. Mature trees were snapped and fully de-leafed with numerous large branches broken off. The tornado then continued to the north-northeast and twisted multiple smaller metal high-voltage power towers. Further to the north-northeast before it lifted, the tornado struck another farmstead where a farm outbuilding was destroyed, the shingles, siding, and gutters were severely damaged on a house, and trees were snapped about half way up. Based on the damage done this tornado was rated EF-1, and from that wind speeds were estimated to be around 110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours over central North Dakota in a very unstable environment, with several becoming supercells. Initially, very large hail was the main threat, but as the episode continued, tornadic development became the primary concern from any storms, with a total of nine tornadoes occurring. The first tornado touched down southwest of Hannover in Oliver County, and less than an hour later another tornado developed north of Crown Butte Lake in northeast Morton County. Then, after approximately an hour and a half, seven tornadoes were reported in the span of one hour, from around 10:12 to 11:12 PM CDT, in Burleigh, Kidder, and Emmons Counties. The most damaging tornado, which was rated an EF-2, impacted a farmstead west of Temvik in Emmons County causing significant damage. Two notable tornadoes touched down just to the north of the city of Bismarck which were highly visible, but one only caused limited damage. In addition to the tornadoes, these storms produced very large hail. The largest hail reported was 3.5 inches in diameter in Oliver County near Hannover, which fell from the same storm that produced the first tornado in Oliver County. North of Bismarck, extensive damage occurred due to hail up to the size of baseballs falling in multiple rural subdivisions earlier in the evening before the tornadic storm arrived. Many hail reports were received from the ICECHIP (In-situ Collaborative Experiment for the Collection of Hail In the Plains) project in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused significant crop damage to parts of northern Towner County during the late afternoon and early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail estimated in size. Significant crop  damage occurred in near mature wheat and  soybeans.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A QLCS type mesoscale convective system brought widespread severe thunderstorm winds to eastern ND, along with, multiple brief tornadoes during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was likely rain wrapped intermittently produced EF-1 damage across its nearly 4 mile long path with wind speeds up to 110mph.  The tornado initially touched down about 1.5 miles northwest of the Grand Forks International Airport tracking northeast and eventually crossing I-29 before lifting just before the Red River of the North. A mobile home was rolled on its side with a person inside that needed rescued, multiple homes garages were destroyed, and wood poles were damaged on electrical transmission lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A QLCS type mesoscale convective system brought widespread severe thunderstorm winds to eastern ND, along with, multiple brief tornadoes during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly developed in east Mekinock about 1.9 miles northeast of the Grand Forks Air Force Base. It caused damage to the garages of 3 different homes, with a maximum expected wind of 105 mph based on one garage that was completely destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A QLCS type mesoscale convective system brought widespread severe thunderstorm winds to eastern ND, along with, multiple brief tornadoes during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado likely intermittent and rain wrapped destroyed 2 grain bins and produced shelterbelt tree damage along its nearly 3 mile long path near the community of McCanna approximately 8 miles northwest of Larimore. Peak wind speeds are estimated at105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A QLCS type mesoscale convective system brought widespread severe thunderstorm winds to eastern ND, along with, multiple brief tornadoes during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief rain wrapped tornado developed within the squall line and produced EF-0 damage to trees near a farmstead and EF-1 tree damage to the eastern side of a shelterbelt about 2 miles south of Larimore. Peak estimated wind speed was 95 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the second event entry for one tornado that passed through two counties, Kidder and Stutsman. This Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) tornado started northwest of Crystal Springs in Kidder County and moved to the east-southeast. The tornado then shifted towards the east-northeast near the Stutsman County line, and continued into Stutsman County before it lifted to the north-northwest of Medina. Near the point of touchdown, a machine shed was completely destroyed, with the roof laying in a pasture about 350 feet away. Approximately five miles to the south-southeast, two additional farmsteads in close proximity were struck. The first one had an older barn along with a pole barn completely destroyed. The second farmstead had an older barn collapse, and an empty 18 foot diameter grain bin lifted and moved approximately 400 feet. Along the grain bin's path it hit a building, two vehicles, and a camper before it came to rest in front of the collapsed barn.  Shortly thereafter, the tornado turned north-northeast and then struck another farmstead approximately 3 miles away. At this farm, a quonset building was moved off its foundation, and boards from the inside were sucked out and laying a few feet away. Additional buildings were damaged, with debris from a children's play house blown a half mile away and a picnic table blown a quarter mile. Mature trees were snapped and fully de-leafed with numerous large branches broken off. The tornado then continued to the north-northeast and twisted multiple smaller metal high-voltage power towers. Further to the north-northeast before it lifted, the tornado struck another farmstead where a farm outbuilding was destroyed, the shingles, siding, and gutters were severely damaged on a house, and trees were snapped about half way up. Based on the damage done this tornado was rated EF-1, and from that wind speeds were estimated to be around 110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours over central North Dakota in a very unstable environment, with several becoming supercells. Initially, very large hail was the main threat, but as the episode continued, tornadic development became the primary concern from any storms, with a total of nine tornadoes occurring. The first tornado touched down southwest of Hannover in Oliver County, and less than an hour later another tornado developed north of Crown Butte Lake in northeast Morton County. Then, after approximately an hour and a half, seven tornadoes were reported in the span of one hour, from around 10:12 to 11:12 PM CDT, in Burleigh, Kidder, and Emmons Counties. The most damaging tornado, which was rated an EF-2, impacted a farmstead west of Temvik in Emmons County causing significant damage. Two notable tornadoes touched down just to the north of the city of Bismarck which were highly visible, but one only caused limited damage. In addition to the tornadoes, these storms produced very large hail. The largest hail reported was 3.5 inches in diameter in Oliver County near Hannover, which fell from the same storm that produced the first tornado in Oliver County. North of Bismarck, extensive damage occurred due to hail up to the size of baseballs falling in multiple rural subdivisions earlier in the evening before the tornadic storm arrived. Many hail reports were received from the ICECHIP (In-situ Collaborative Experiment for the Collection of Hail In the Plains) project in the area.

The highest measured wind gust associated with the derecho was 101 mph at a North Dakota Agricultural Weather Network (NDAWN) station 5 miles northwest of Linton. There were also several tornadoes produced by the intense line of storms, including two tornadoes rated as EF-1 (north of Interstate 94 between Crystal Springs and Medina near the Kidder/Stutsman County Line, and to the southwest of Grace City in Foster County), and one tornado rated as EF-2 (north of Sweet Briar Lake in Morton County).  The derecho resulted in one injury which occurred in a housing development to the south of Lincoln in Burleigh County as a male was outdoors and was struck by flying debris. Also, one indirect fatality occurred near Courtenay in Stutsman County where a 76-year-old male died in his sleep from his breathing device not operating properly due to the power outage caused by the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down to the southwest of Grace City, then tracked northeasterly approximately five and three-quarter miles before lifting. Near the point of touchdown, the tornado struck a farmstead causing significant damage. A barn which was built with brick about half-way up then built with wood and sheet metal had the top wooden and sheet metal portion torn off, while an adjacent silo had its top blown off. A pole barn had its roof blown off and another barn had its doors blown off. The tornado then progressed through open fields until it moved through a shelterbelt at a farmstead near the end of its path, where it broke many trees, and twisted off large branches. It also took one large branch and speared it into a camper. Across the road the tornado broke down power lines before it broke in the large door and tore a significant portion off one of the Foster County shop buildings housing a maintainer. Based on the damage done this tornado was rated EF-1, and from that wind speeds were estimated to be around 110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outbreak of severe thunderstorms brought extreme winds, very large hail, and multiple tornadoes across much of southern North Dakota from the late afternoon into the nighttime hours. The lowest layer of the atmosphere (up to 1 mile above the surface) also contained strong wind shear, which was one of the key ingredients that resulted in the higher risk for strong tornadoes. This event has been classified as a derecho, a term that is reserved for the most intense, widespread, and long-lasting lines of severe thunderstorms. Meanwhile, a supercell thunderstorm developed just south of Jamestown, and produced three-inch diameter hail as it moved northward into the city. This storm then slowly moved to the east-northeast and produced a brief tornado between Jamestown and Spiritwood. Shortly thereafter, a larger tornado formed near Spiritwood and moved to the east-northeast into Barnes County where EF-3 damage occurred, before tracking back into Stutsman County while it was dissipating. Specific details on the damage with this event can be found in the National Weather Service-Grand Forks Storm Data entry. Another supercell thunderstorm then formed south of the initial storm, moving southeast through southern Stutsman and northern LaMoure Counties. This storm produced a tornado near Adrian and another north of Verona. During this time, the line of storms to the west continued to strengthen, producing a wide swath of 80 to 100 mph wind gusts as it moved through south central North Dakota and across the James River Valley during the mid-evening into the nighttime hours. Also, one indirect fatality occurred near Courtenay in Stutsman County where a 76-year-old male died in his sleep from his breathing device not operating properly due to the power outage caused by the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jamestown area was struck by the derecho during the late night hours, with estimated winds around 90 mph. Many trees throughout the city were blown over, causing additional damage to areas that were struck by very large hail earlier in the evening. Lakeside campground to the north of the city had to be closed for an extended period of time due to widespread tree damage and broken water lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours over central North Dakota in a very unstable environment, with several becoming supercells. Initially, very large hail was the main threat, but as the episode continued, tornadic development became the primary concern from any storms, with a total of nine tornadoes occurring. The first tornado touched down southwest of Hannover in Oliver County, and less than an hour later another tornado developed north of Crown Butte Lake in northeast Morton County. Then, after approximately an hour and a half, seven tornadoes were reported in the span of one hour, from around 10:12 to 11:12 PM CDT, in Burleigh, Kidder, and Emmons Counties. The most damaging tornado, which was rated an EF-2, impacted a farmstead west of Temvik in Emmons County causing significant damage. Two notable tornadoes touched down just to the north of the city of Bismarck which were highly visible, but one only caused limited damage. In addition to the tornadoes, these storms produced very large hail. The largest hail reported was 3.5 inches in diameter in Oliver County near Hannover, which fell from the same storm that produced the first tornado in Oliver County. North of Bismarck, extensive damage occurred due to hail up to the size of baseballs falling in multiple rural subdivisions earlier in the evening before the tornadic storm arrived. Many hail reports were received from the ICECHIP (In-situ Collaborative Experiment for the Collection of Hail In the Plains) project in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) tornado occurred to the north-northwest of Sweet Briar Lake. Three farmsteads were impacted. The first farmstead had minimal damage. The second farmstead had two large pole barns lose their roofs, with some collapsed walls. A half-full steel grain bin had its top completely removed, with the bin side dented in to grain level. A large Harvestore silo was significantly dented. A mile of power poles were broken between the second and third farms. The third farm had damage to the house which included much of the shingling being removed and a portion of the roof sheathing being removed, along with windows being broken. In addition, a pole barn had almost all its roof covering blown off with mainly rafters remaining. After the third farm, the tornado then struck a wind turbine, which snapped its support pole 1/4 of the way up, resulting the turbine toppling. Another turbine suffered blade damage. Based on the damage done this tornado was rated EF-2, and from that wind speeds were estimated to be around 120 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short-wave trough approached western and central North Dakota where strong instability and elevated deep-layer shear were noted. Initial thunderstorms developed over northwest North Dakota over Burke and Divide Counties during the evening hours, producing wind gusts to 60 mph and hail up to the size of half-dollars. Overnight, a second round of thunderstorms moved into southwest North Dakota from Montana producing strong winds. The Hettinger area of Adams County experienced the most extensive damage from these winds, with 94 mph wind gusts measured at the Hettinger NDAWN. About two miles south of Hettinger, a shop building was severely damage, with two of the four walls collapsed and the roof completely removed. Within the city of Hettinger, many campers and trailers were rolled onto their side, and multiple trees were down. Power lines were also down in various locations due to both the strong winds and trees falling on the lines. The storms then continued to move to the east through the remainder of the overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two pole barn structures were severely damaged by thunderstorm wind gusts. One structure had the complete roof destroyed along with one of the walls, while the other had sections of the roof blown off and collapse. Machinery and a camper inside the structures were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm formed ahead of a modest short wave trough and moved through McHenry County, with estimated 90 mph thunderstorm wind gusts causing extensive damage to the grain elevator at Voltaire. Two large grain bins were blown completely over with other bins damaged. In addition, the grain leg connecting all the bins from the main terminal was blown off. The thunderstorm then weakened as it moved off to the southeast, but additional storms developed along its outflow. One of these storms became severe over Kidder County, producing 70 mph wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive damage occurred to the grain elevator at Voltaire. Two large grain bins were blown completely over with other bins damaged. In addition, the grain leg connecting all the bins from the main terminal was blown off.

The tornado then crossed a road and knocked over a power pole before impacting the farmstead. The home suffered damage to the siding and roof, along with a slight shift in the foundation. The tornado also destroyed a few outbuildings anchored with chains, and significantly damaged a large quonset building. In addition, a large stainless-steel flatbed trailer was tossed around 250 yards, and another smaller trailer was tossed around 350 yards before landing in a grove of trees. The tornado continued to move to the northeast, snapping some hardwood trees in a shelterbelt before lifting. Based on the damage done this tornado was rated EF-1, and from that wind speeds were estimated to be around 110 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines down in Mayville.  Winds estimated at 75 mph. Time estimated  via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An environment with elevated instability and very strong deep layer shear set the stage for severe weather development over western and south central North Dakota. A long lived supercell thunderstorm developed over far southwest North Dakota near a modest frontal boundary, and quickly became severe over Billings County. The storm then continued to track to the east-southeast producing very large damaging hail as it moved through Stark, Hettinger, and Grant Counties. The largest hail reported was three inches in diameter which fell south of Dickinson in Stark County. EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the second of two tornadoes to impact the Temvik area. The tornado developed just to the south of a farmstead and moved north, impacting the farmstead. The tornado picked up a large horse trailer, and tossed it approximately 150 yards into a tree. The tornado then destroyed a new large metal workshop, a small garage, and a large wooden barn. The workshop and garage had both been anchored into concrete slabs. The tornado also impacted the house by blowing off a well-built front porch and causing significant roof covering damage. Multiple hardwood and softwood trees were severely damaged with some snapped. The tornado then tracked slowly to the north through open fields before dissipating. Based on the damage done this tornado was rated EF-2, and from that wind speeds were estimated to be around 120 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outbreak of severe thunderstorms brought extreme winds, very large hail, and multiple tornadoes across much of southern North Dakota from the late afternoon into the nighttime hours. A low-pressure system in southeastern Montana and a warm front stretching eastward across southern North Dakota provided a focus for the severe thunderstorms. Several environmental parameters were in place for a significant outbreak, most notable was the amount of deep-layer wind shear. The lowest layer of the atmosphere (up to 1 mile above the surface) also contained strong wind shear, which was one of the key ingredients that resulted in the higher risk for strong tornadoes. This event has been classified as a derecho, a term that is reserved for the most intense, widespread, and long-lasting lines of severe thunderstorms. By definition, a derecho is a widespread severe windstorm characterized by a family of destructive downbursts containing multiple 75+ mph gusts associated with the system.|Thunderstorms initially developed in eastern Montana in the late afternoon and moved into southwest North Dakota. Severe wind gusts around 60 mph were reported in far southwest North Dakota from Dickinson to Bowman during the early evening. Thereafter, storms grew into a pronounced line as they moved east and began accelerating. Shortly thereafter, extremely strong wind gusts started occurring, including a measured 94 mph gust to the northwest of Elgin in Grant County, and estimated 95 mph wind gusts in Hettinger County where a farmstead was severely damaged. Meanwhile, a supercell thunderstorm developed just south of Jamestown, and produced three-inch diameter hail as it moved northward into the city. This storm then slowly moved to the east-northeast and produced a brief tornado between Jamestown and Spiritwood. Shortly thereafter, a larger tornado formed near Spiritwood and moved to the east-northeast into Barnes County where EF-3 damage occurred, before tracking back into Stutsman County while it was dissipating. Specific details on the damage with this event can be found in the National Weather Service-Grand Forks Storm Data entry. Another supercell thunderstorm then formed south of the initial storm, moving southeast through southern Stutsman and northern LaMoure Counties. This storm produced a tornado near Adrian and another north of Verona. During this time, the line of storms to the west continued to strengthen, producing a wide swath of 80 to 100 mph wind gusts as it moved through south central North Dakota and across the James River Valley during the mid-evening into the nighttime hours. The derecho caused extensive tree and structure damage along its path, with significant damage to grain storage equipment and power transmission poles. The highest measured wind gust associated with the derecho was 101 mph at a North Dakota Agricultural Weather Network (NDAWN) station 5 miles northwest of Linton. There were also several tornadoes produced by the intense line of storms, including two tornadoes rated as EF-1 (north of Interstate 94 between Crystal Springs and Medina near the Kidder/Stutsman County Line, and to the southwest of Grace City in Foster County), and one tornado rated as EF-2 (north of Sweet Briar Lake in Morton County).  The derecho resulted in one injury which occurred in a housing development to the south of Lincoln in Burleigh County as a male was outdoors and was struck by flying debris. Also, one indirect fatality occurred near Courtenay in Stutsman County where a 76-year-old male died in his sleep from his breathing device not operating properly due to the power outage caused by the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds pushed through the Copper Ridge subdivision south of Lincoln, and caused extensive damage. Several home were severely damaged, with large sections of shingles blown off roofs, some roof sheathing blown off, blown in garage doors, extensive siding damage, and removed decking. Several detached garage and smaller shop buildings experienced similar damage, with one experiencing its side wall being blown out. A large shop building was completely destroyed with the damage blowing across the road into the neighbors property. One injury occurred when a male was outdoors chasing his sunglasses that were blown away in the wind when an airborne rafter struck him in the shin causing a severe leg injury. Winds from this event were estimated at 110 mph.

Then, after approximately an hour and a half, seven tornadoes were reported in the span of one hour, from around 10:12 to 11:12 PM CDT, in Burleigh, Kidder, and Emmons Counties. The most damaging tornado, which was rated an EF-2, impacted a farmstead west of Temvik in Emmons County causing significant damage. Two notable tornadoes touched down just to the north of the city of Bismarck which were highly visible, but one only caused limited damage. In addition to the tornadoes, these storms produced very large hail. The largest hail reported was 3.5 inches in diameter in Oliver County near Hannover, which fell from the same storm that produced the first tornado in Oliver County. North of Bismarck, extensive damage occurred due to hail up to the size of baseballs falling in multiple rural subdivisions earlier in the evening before the tornadic storm arrived. Many hail reports were received from the ICECHIP (In-situ Collaborative Experiment for the Collection of Hail In the Plains) project in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado formed in an open field east of Menoken and moved southeast over Interstate 94 before dissipating. The tornado impacted no structures, thus there was no damage to evaluate. By National Weather Service policy the tornado is rated EF-U (Unknown).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An environment with elevated instability and very strong deep layer shear set the stage for severe weather development over western and south central North Dakota. A long lived supercell thunderstorm developed over far southwest North Dakota near a modest frontal boundary, and quickly became severe over Billings County. The storm then continued to track to the east-southeast producing very large damaging hail as it moved through Stark, Hettinger, and Grant Counties. The largest hail reported was three inches in diameter which fell south of Dickinson in Stark County. In addition, a tornado developed approximately 9 miles southwest of Dickinson in Stark County, and traveled to the south approximately 1.4 miles. Two houses along the path of the tornado had roofs partially blown off and some siding ripped off. Tree damage also occurred, with one tree having an eight inch diameter branch snap off. Fences were broken down and two power poles were broken. Based on the damage done this tornado was rated EF-1, and from that wind speeds were estimated to be around 100 mph. Additional thunderstorms developed and moved through central and western North Dakota through the evening. One of these storms became severe over south central North Dakota producing one-inch diameter hail in Logan County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rainfall over much of western and central North Dakota led to significant drought improvement. During the first week of June, most locations with a drought designation saw a one-category improvement in drought intensity. By mid-June, almost all locations were designated D1 (Moderate Drought) or better. Only a tiny sliver of very far western Golden Valley County remained in D2 (severe drought) designation, with the mass majority of the county in D1.

A strong short-wave trough approached western and central North Dakota where strong instability and elevated deep-layer shear were noted. Initial thunderstorms developed over northwest North Dakota over Burke and Divide Counties during the evening hours, producing wind gusts to 60 mph and hail up to the size of half-dollars. Overnight, a second round of thunderstorms moved into southwest North Dakota from Montana producing strong winds. The Hettinger area of Adams County experienced the most extensive damage from these winds, with 94 mph wind gusts measured at the Hettinger NDAWN. About two miles south of Hettinger, a shop building was severely damage, with two of the four walls collapsed and the roof completely removed. Within the city of Hettinger, many campers and trailers were rolled onto their side, and multiple trees were down. Power lines were also down in various locations due to both the strong winds and trees falling on the lines. The storms then continued to move to the east through the remainder of the overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a cold front that stretched from northeast towards south-central North Dakota, in an area of elevated instability. A storm became severe in the mid-afternoon producing half-dollar size hail near the community of McHenry in Foster County. Later in the afternoon, another thunderstorm developed over Foster County. From this storm, two landspout tornadoes developed nearly simultaneously  to the east of the community of Bordulac. The tornadoes were on the ground together for a period of time. Both tornadoes touched down in an open field and impacted no structures. There was no damage to evaluate. By National Weather Service policy the tornadoes were rated EF-U (Unknown).

Strong non-convective winds which were caused by a wake low behind a departing complex of severe thunderstorms, caused damage over far southwest North Dakota. Within the city of Bowman, estimated winds of 75 mph blew the head house off an older grain elevator, blew over a large evergreen tree at the courthouse, and blew down various tree branches around the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Within the city of Bowman, the head house on an older grain elevator blew off, a large evergreen tree blew over at the courthouse, and various tree branches blew down around the city.

An isolated severe thunderstorm formed ahead of a modest short wave trough and moved through McHenry County, with estimated 90 mph thunderstorm wind gusts causing extensive damage to the grain elevator at Voltaire. Two large grain bins were blown completely over with other bins damaged. In addition, the grain leg connecting all the bins from the main terminal was blown off. The thunderstorm then weakened as it moved off to the southeast, but additional storms developed along its outflow. One of these storms became severe over Kidder County, producing 70 mph wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed over southwest North Dakota as a compact short-wave trough moved through the area. A short lived landspout tornado developed north of Hettinger in Adams County. The tornado touched down in an open field  and impacted no structures. There was no damage to evaluate. By National Weather Service policy the tornado is rated EF-U (Unknown).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down in an open field north of Hettinger and impacted no structures. There was no damage to evaluate. By National Weather Service policy the tornado is rated EF-U (Unknown).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms including supercells produced large hail, damaging winds and tornadoes across parts of eastern North Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large dusty tornado was reported near Calio ND with no damage conveyed from any sources who witnessed it. This tornado started in Towner County and traveled 0.50 miles to the Cavalier county line, where it traveled an additional 1.04 miles.  It is suspected that this was spawned out of land spout processes which likely contributed to its dusty tubelike appearance as seen on social media. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Friday, June 20th into the early morning hours of June 21st, much of the region was affected by severe weather. Supercell thunderstorms developed across southeast North Dakota ahead of an approaching convective complex, producing multiple strong, destructive, and fatal tornadoes. This was followed by a powerful line of severe thunderstorms categorized as a derecho, which tracked across much of the region resulting in widespread straight line wind damage. After the derecho passed through the region, there was a period of prolonged destructive winds that affected portions of central and east central North Dakota, as well as adjacent portions of Minnesota. This was a unique event due to the combination of the violent tornadoes ahead of the derecho, and the long duration of significant winds that followed the derecho. Based on estimates provided by North Dakota Agriculture Commissioner Doug Goehring, the state lost anywhere from 50 to 80 million bushels of grain storage capacity from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ground survey and satellite imagery confirm a tornado tracked intermittently for approximately 5 miles. 2 farmsteads sustained damage to buildings both suggesting winds around 100mph in the first couple miles of the tornado there after satellite imagery shows scouring in fields for another 3 miles or so where at one point it reached 800 yards wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Friday, June 20th into the early morning hours of June 21st, much of the region was affected by severe weather. Supercell thunderstorms developed across southeast North Dakota ahead of an approaching convective complex, producing multiple strong, destructive, and fatal tornadoes. This was followed by a powerful line of severe thunderstorms categorized as a derecho, which tracked across much of the region resulting in widespread straight line wind damage. After the derecho passed through the region, there was a period of prolonged destructive winds that affected portions of central and east central North Dakota, as well as adjacent portions of Minnesota. Based on estimates provided by North Dakota Agriculture Commissioner Doug Goehring, the state lost anywhere from 50 to 80 million bushels of grain storage capacity from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: First visible indication of the tornado occurred at 1017pm. The tornado tracked NE for about 3.3 miles from its start doing mainly tree and power line damage. It did do some EF1 roof damage to one farmstead in this initial phase. The tornado tracked across 117th Ave and damaged 3 power poles. Lifted north tracking over open fields which showed some ground scouring via satellite imagery. Then the tornado struck a farmstead from the southeast and spread the debris to the north/Northwest. The tornado continued to the northwest crossing 117th and taking out a another power pole as it roped out. This tornado's path was about 5.2 miles long and 400 yards wide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed in central Barnes County 5 miles southwest of Valley City and tracked southeast, caused EF-0 and EF-1 damage to trees and several homes. This tornado was almost 6 miles long.

After the derecho passed through the region, there was a period of prolonged destructive winds that affected portions of central and east central North Dakota, as well as adjacent portions of Minnesota. This was a unique event due to the combination of the violent tornadoes ahead of the derecho, and the long duration of significant winds that followed the derecho. Based on estimates provided by North Dakota Agriculture Commissioner Doug Goehring, the state lost anywhere from 50 to 80 million bushels of grain storage capacity from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet observations confirmed a 74 mph wind gust 2 miles SE of Blanchard. Mesonet station DVI12057 Blanchard ND.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado initially developed in far eastern Stutsman County about 2 miles east-southeast of Spiritwood, and immediately tracked northeast into Barnes County. As it continued northeast it missed the community of Urbana in Barnes County to the northwest. As it continued this northeast track it caused EF-3 damage|to a metal building structure and homestead. Additional EF-2 damage occurred at that home, including the loss of an upper floor and garage. Several nearby Morton buildings were also destroyed next to the MBS. At least 3 vehicles and a trailer were lofted by the tornado from that home, along with the engine block of a dump truck. The vehicle remnants were thrown or deposited between 0.2 and 0.25 miles along an arc from the south to southeast. One vehicle was a 1980 Chevy pickup truck that had originally been located between a backhoe and dump truck by the house. The vehicles cannot currently be used as DI's under the current EF scale, however they could be reviewed in future expansions of the EF scale. The tornado continued on a north to northeast track and 1  mile from the first home caused EF-3 damage to another home along with EF-3 damage to nearby trees. The tornado then turned northwest causing visible ground scouring as it eventually turned west back into Stutsman county as it weakened and eventually dissipated in eastern Stutsman County. High resolution Worldview 2 satellite imagery reveals trackable ground scouring showing this turning track up to around 1.25 miles west of the Barnes County and Stutsman County border.
